caveats: a: OEWS publishes the relevant occupation mix only for the broader 524120 group, not 524128.
a: Occupation shares are not task shares, and mean wages only imperfectly represent compensation weights.
a: The estimate is gross technical exposure, not realized headcount elimination.
rho: NAIC adoption surveys cover adjacent major insurance lines and generally larger respondents, not warranty carriers.
rho: Use, planned use, and exploration are broader than successful task implementation.
rho: Regulatory expectations could either slow deployment or accelerate standardized governed adoption.
e: The estimate cannot verify how many injected firms are captives, affiliates, or inactive shells.
e: Revenue does not map cleanly to premium volume or normalized carrier EBITDA.
e: The narrowed lens excludes public or quasi-public deposit/share insurers for coherence.
s5: The transaction source has no target-population denominator and is not specific to 524128.
s5: Announced deals are not identical to completed changes of control.
s5: Small private transactions may be underreported while public deals skew large.
q: No public study isolates five-year AI benefit retention for warranty carriers.
q: Loss-ratio volatility can mask operating savings.
q: Partner concentration and contract structure can dominate retention at an individual target.
d5: The output series is for all insurance carriers, not 524128.
d5: The employment projection is not a direct quantity-demand measure.
d5: Premium growth can reflect insured values or pricing rather than real service quantity.
o: The construct concerns continued need for an operator, not preservation of every current job.
o: Shared-services platforms can eliminate standalone functions without eliminating the carrier entity.
o: State-specific requirements and captive structures vary.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The carrier earns recurring premiums or economically similar underwriting revenue from consumers, manufacturers, retailers, dealers, or affinity partners, assumes insured risk, pays covered claims, and retains underwriting and investment economics after claims, commissions, and operating expenses.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence to privately controlled commercial warranty and other direct carriers. The residual NAICS definition also contains public or quasi-public deposit/share insurers whose mandate, ownership, and transfer market are not comparable to an LMM commercial acquisition target.
lens.excluded_activities: Government or quasi-government bank-deposit and credit-union share insurers; life, health, property-casualty, and title carriers classified elsewhere; insurance agencies, brokerages, third-party administrators, and non-insurance service-contract administrators.
lens.included_activities: Privately controlled direct carriers underwriting recurring product-warranty and other non-life, non-health insurance, with emphasis on policy administration, underwriting, claims, fraud review, customer service, compliance, and partner-account management.

a.bridge: May 2023 OEWS data for the broader 524120 direct-carrier group show 37.39% of employment in business and financial operations, 25.78% in office and administrative support, and 10.76% in computer and mathematical work; claims adjusters alone are 16.45%, insurance underwriters 8.28%, claims/policy-processing clerks 9.21%, and customer-service representatives 6.07%. I translate that digital, document-heavy occupation mix into the wage-weighted share of current tasks technically addressable by AI or workflow automation, while excluding already automated activity and judgment-intensive or regulated final decisions. Warranty carriers may have simpler claims than the broad group, biasing the base downward if their workflows are more standardized, but the broad group also includes technology-intensive carriers, biasing it upward.
caveats: OEWS publishes the relevant occupation mix only for the broader 524120 group, not 524128.
Occupation shares are not task shares, and mean wages only imperfectly represent compensation weights.
The estimate is gross technical exposure, not realized headcount elimination.
a.change_evidence: A 524128-specific payroll-by-occupation table, task time study, or insurer operating-process benchmark separating already automated from still-manual work would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Policy intake, document classification, coverage checks, claim triage, fraud flags, correspondence, customer support, renewal review, and analyst preparation are materially automatable or augmentable. Complex claim exceptions, actuarial judgment, regulatory filings, partner negotiations, and accountable underwriting decisions remain labor-intensive.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for the broad insurance-carrier industry (524100) from 724.0 in 2024 to 870.0 in 2034, a 20.2% ten-year increase; applying the implied constant growth rate for five years gives approximately 1.096. BLS separately projects employment in 524120 direct non-life/non-health carriers from 678.5 thousand to 695.9 thousand, supporting durable rather than collapsing activity. I round the five-year output factor to 1.10. Warranty demand can diverge with durable-goods sales, product reliability, and embedded-service-contract regulation, so the range spans mild contraction to stronger growth.
caveats: The output series is for all insurance carriers, not 524128.
The employment projection is not a direct quantity-demand measure.
Premium growth can reflect insured values or pricing rather than real service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price five-year revenue or premium forecast specific to U.S. warranty insurance, including policy counts, average insured value, cancellation rates, and claims-quality adjustments, would supersede the broad carrier proxy.
d5.rationale: Insurance demand is recurring and tied to installed products and partner distribution, while digital commerce and higher repair complexity can support warranty coverage. Cyclical durable-goods sales, product quality, self-insurance, and consumer-protection changes limit certainty.

caveats: The estimate cannot verify how many injected firms are captives, affiliates, or inactive shells.
Revenue does not map cleanly to premium volume or normalized carrier EBITDA.
The narrowed lens excludes public or quasi-public deposit/share insurers for coherence.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 66 injected candidates showing ownership, premium mix, regulatory status, revenue, EBITDA, and whether each is a transferable operating carrier would directly replace this estimate.
e.rationale: Most private firms counted in the injected LMM universe should be commercial warranty or specialty direct carriers compatible with the narrowed lens, but some may be captives, affiliated risk vehicles, dormant entities, unusually capital-intensive carriers, or businesses whose economics do not resemble a recurring operating company. The NAICS definition's inclusion of deposit/share insurers creates additional classification uncertainty even though government establishments should generally not enter the private-firm denominator.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 524128 ($1,923,123,197) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 524128 ($23,515,805,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2357 (margins.json[524]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Insurance (General) EBITDA/Sales 23.57%); revenue/employee $723,274 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 524128); class <5: 303 firms, avg revenue/firm $569,904 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $134,326 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,976,925 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $465,961 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,507,438 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,533,803 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $27,544,088 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,492,142 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $156,415,909 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,867,230 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 39 firms, avg revenue/firm $522,385,513 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $123,126,265 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 66

o.bridge: NAIC states that insurers remain responsible for compliance when using AI, that human oversight remains important, and that underwriters, claims professionals, agents, and customer-service staff continue to exercise judgment. I apply that sector-wide regulatory and operating logic to warranty carriers: technology can automate workflows, but an accountable licensed risk-bearing operator must still set policy, hold capital, manage partners and exceptions, oversee models, and meet claims obligations. The residual chance below one allows highly standardized books to be absorbed into a platform with minimal standalone management.
caveats: The construct concerns continued need for an operator, not preservation of every current job.
Shared-services platforms can eliminate standalone functions without eliminating the carrier entity.
State-specific requirements and captive structures vary.
o.change_evidence: Regulatory approval of substantially autonomous carrier operations, evidence of platform roll-ups eliminating nearly all target-level operating roles, or target-specific outsourcing maps could materially change the share.
o.rationale: AI changes the labor and process design of a warranty carrier but does not itself supply regulated risk-bearing capacity, capital allocation, governance, distribution relationships, or accountability for coverage and claims decisions.

caveats: No public study isolates five-year AI benefit retention for warranty carriers.
Loss-ratio volatility can mask operating savings.
Partner concentration and contract structure can dominate retention at an individual target.
q.change_evidence: Target-level renewal histories, affinity-contract repricing clauses, expense-ratio benchmarking, competitor pricing, and documented post-automation margin realization would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: A carrier can initially retain savings through lower claims-handling expense, improved fraud detection, better loss selection, and faster service, but manufacturers, retailers, dealers, consumers, reinsurers, and regulators can capture part of the gain through commissions, pricing, contract renewals, service requirements, or capital expectations. Proprietary data, embedded distribution relationships, and switching costs support some retention; transparent pricing and partner bargaining constrain it.

rho.bridge: NAIC reports that 88% of surveyed private-passenger-auto insurers and 70% of surveyed homeowners insurers use, plan to use, or plan to explore AI/ML, while the homeowners survey found use across claims, underwriting, marketing, fraud, and rating. I convert this adoption-or-intent evidence into the share of addressable warranty-carrier tasks implemented within five years, discounting for the survey's larger regulated carriers, the inclusion of plans and exploration rather than production use, legacy-system integration, model governance, and the smaller resources of LMM targets. The five-year horizon and growing third-party tool supply partially offset those discounts.
caveats: NAIC adoption surveys cover adjacent major insurance lines and generally larger respondents, not warranty carriers.
Use, planned use, and exploration are broader than successful task implementation.
Regulatory expectations could either slow deployment or accelerate standardized governed adoption.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal 524128 or warranty-insurer survey reporting production AI coverage by workflow and firm size, or audited implementation roadmaps for representative targets, could move the base materially.
rho.rationale: Claims and policy workflows are structured and vendor-addressable, but production deployment is slower than technical feasibility because carrier systems, data quality, state oversight, testing, and human-review controls constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Deloitte's SNL-based U.S. and Bermuda insurance-underwriter series records 43 announced underwriting deals in 2024 and 44 in 2025, including 32 and 26 P&C deals respectively. With no disclosed denominator of independent carriers, I use the persistence of annual carrier dealmaking only as evidence that control transfers are feasible, then apply a conservative five-year probability to an eligible private LMM warranty carrier. The published transactions skew larger and broader than this niche, which can overstate transferability; private small-company deals omitted from public databases can understate it.
caveats: The transaction source has no target-population denominator and is not specific to 524128.
Announced deals are not identical to completed changes of control.
Small private transactions may be underreported while public deals skew large.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census for U.S. warranty and residual specialty direct carriers, matched to an independent-firm denominator and including private deals, would materially tighten this probability.
s5.rationale: Carrier acquisitions occur regularly, but regulatory approvals, capital requirements, reserve and claims diligence, affinity-partner consent, captive ownership, and strategic-parent structures make a sale less routine than in asset-light insurance distribution.

principal_driver: A concentrated digital workflow mix in claims, underwriting, policy processing, and customer service creates meaningful scope for governed AI augmentation and automation.
principal_weakness: The investable universe is small and poorly observed, with no niche-specific transaction denominator and substantial heterogeneity in ownership, capital structure, and regulatory profile.

run_meta.title: Other Direct Insurance (except Life, Health, and Medical) Carriers

sources.supports: Industry boundary, direct underwriting role, and illustrative inclusion of product-warranty and deposit/share insurance carriers; supports the heterogeneity finding and narrowed lens.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 524128 Other Direct Insurance (except Life, Health, and Medical) Carriers

sources.supports: Broader-industry occupational employment shares and wages, including claims adjusters, underwriters, claims/policy clerks, customer service, administrative, financial, and technology occupations used to proxy task exposure.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Direct Insurance (except Life, Health, and Medical) Carriers

sources.supports: BLS identifies increased use of AI-enhanced software to automate claims and policy processing as a factor reducing claims and policy processing clerks in 524120.
sources.title: BLS Employment Projections: Factors affecting occupational utilization

sources.supports: Insurance AI use cases, line-of-business adoption evidence, third-party model use, continued insurer legal responsibility, and the continuing role of human judgment and oversight.
sources.title: NAIC Insurance Topics: Artificial Intelligence

sources.supports: Survey evidence on AI/ML adoption and usage across claims, underwriting, marketing, fraud, rating, and loss prevention, including the predominance of augmentation or support in important claims decisions.
sources.title: NAIC Homeowners Insurance AI/ML Survey analysis

sources.supports: Broad insurance-carrier real-output projection and 524120 employment projection used to proxy five-year real demand durability.
sources.title: BLS Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034

sources.supports: SNL-based 2024-2025 U.S. and Bermuda insurance-underwriter and P&C transaction counts used only as a broad feasibility proxy for control transfers.
sources.title: Deloitte 2026 Insurance M&A Outlook

title: Other Direct Insurance (except Life, Health, and Medical) Carriers
